Core slow‑cooker habits
- Brown meat first when a recipe calls for it (better flavor and color).  
- Layer root veg (potatoes, carrots) on the bottom and meat on top so everything cooks evenly.  
- Don’t overfill the pot — leave ~1/3 headspace for proper heat circulation.  
- Choose low vs. high based on time: low gives more even, tender results; high shortens time but can tighten proteins.  
- Keep the lid on while cooking; every lift drops temperature and adds time.  
- Add delicate ingredients (fresh herbs, greens, cream, milk, yogurt) near the end so they don’t overcook or separate.  
- Taste and adjust seasoning at the end — slow cooking concentrates flavors and often needs a final splash of acid (vinegar/lemon) or salt.

For vegetarian/vegan dishes
- Slow cookers shine for lentil stews, curries, beans and root-veg braises — they make things tender and flavorful with minimal work.  
- If using dried beans, either pre-soak or use a recipe tested for unsoaked beans (timing matters).

Yogurt and cashew‑based yogurt (you asked about this before)
- Use a thermometer — highly recommended to control temperatures and get consistent yogurt (the slow‑cooker method is less precise without one).  
- If making cashew milk first: strain with cheesecloth or a nut‑milk bag for the smoothest base.  
- For vegan yogurt starters, pick an unflavored, unsweetened starter/alternative so you’re not adding sugars or unexpected flavors.  
- Incubate carefully and then strain/thicken/cool; you can always flavor and sweeten afterward.

Small tweaks that help every recipe
- Follow liquid amounts — too much liquid makes things dilute; too little can scorch.  
- Use the same recipe as a baseline and tweak one variable at a time (time, temp, liquid, salt) so you learn cause→effect.  
- Use a timer and check earlier than you think the first time trying a new recipe — better to catch underdone than overdone.
